figures.gloss: The Sea-Mother who sank to the ocean floor when her own father severed her clinging fingers; each joint became a seal, a walrus, a whale. From her undersea house she withholds or releases all the sea-game, and the angakkuq must descend to comb the filth from her tangled hair before the hunt can resume.
figures.facet: The great-mother as wounded and resentful — provider not by overflowing love but by extracted appeasement; the food-source is a maimed woman who must be soothed, the moral order written into the body of the prey.
figures.provenance: Names and details vary widely across Inuit and Yupik groups (Sedna, Nuliajuk, Takannaaluk, Arnakuagsak); Rasmussen's early-20thC ethnography is the principal but mediated source.

figures.gloss: Father of Sedna and ferryman of the dead, in some accounts the very hand that cut her loose into the sea. He gathers the dead and carries them down to Adlivun, the cold realm beneath the waters and the ice, where they atone before release.
figures.facet: Kinship as cosmology: the underworld is not a stranger's hall but the household of a family broken by a single act of violence — the psychopomp and the sea-goddess are father and daughter, the wound that made the world is domestic.

figures.gloss: Breath, air, weather, and the encompassing intelligence of the world all at once — the medium of life that is also a moral awareness pervading everything. Sila is not prayed to like a person but kept in right relation; a broken taboo troubles the weather, and the storm is the cosmos registering a wrong.
figures.facet: Order as the air itself: where other traditions externalise their law into a king or a written rule, the Inuit locate it in the very breath drawn in and out — sky, sentience, and right-conduct are one indivisible substance.
figures.provenance: Sila (Sila / Silap Inua) is rendered variably as impersonal force, weather-spirit, or near-supreme being across sources; the abstraction is partly a product of translation.

figures.gloss: The shaman, drumming into trance, sends the soul out of the body to journey beneath the sea to Sedna or up through the layers of sky — to comb the goddess's hair, retrieve a stolen soul, or read the cause of sickness and dearth. The descent is dangerous and bodily: passing the guard-dog, the spinning floor, the dark threshold.
figures.facet: The psychopomp inverted — here the living specialist makes the round-trip the dead cannot, a working technology of descent-and-return rather than a one-way ferrying; salvation is a craft, repeatedly performed.

figures.gloss: Each person carries a tarniq, a miniature double of the self that can be lost or stolen, and an atiq, a name-soul passed down so that a dead grandparent lives again in the newborn who bears the name. To name a child is to seat an ancestor's soul within it.
figures.facet: Reincarnation without a wheel: identity is a relay of names, the ancestors continuously re-housed among the living — continuity of the dead achieved not by an afterworld but by the naming of children.

figures.gloss: The helping and harming spirits — tuurngait — that the angakkuq binds as allies, and the master-spirits who own the land-animals as Sedna owns the sea's. The caribou, the bear, the wind each have their keeper; the hunt is a negotiation among owners, not a taking of mere things.
figures.facet: A cosmos with no surplus and no waste: every animal is somebody's, and so every kill is a transaction inside a vast web of consent — the 'one through many' here is ownership, the single rule that all flesh belongs to its master-spirit.

figures.gloss: A sister and brother who lay together in the dark of a winter house; when she smeared his face with soot to know her unseen lover and saw it was her brother, she fled aflame into the sky as the Sun, and he chased her forever as the soot-dark Moon. Their eternal pursuit turns the day and the month.
figures.facet: The luminaries born of incest and shame rather than of sky-marriage — the sun is a fleeing woman and the moon a guilty brother, the heavens set in motion by a transgression the cosmos cannot resolve.

figures.gloss: The name-soul — the inherited identity carried by a personal name. When a child is given the name of the recently dead, the atiq returns: the ancestor lives again through the newborn, who may be addressed with the dead one’s kin-terms.
figures.facet: Personhood as heritable and shared, not singular — you carry whoever’s name you bear. Among the Inuit, naming is a form of return.
figures.provenance: Inuit soul-dualism is well attested (tarniq, the vital double; atiq, the name-soul); see Saladin d’Anglure on the Inuit name and the third sex.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A foundational female figure whose tragic death in or near water generates the spiritual ecology that men must subsequently propitiate.

Both are master-spirits who OWN the land-animals so that every kill is a transaction inside a web of consent; the Inuit game-masters and the Ainu mountain-bear share the ethic that the hunt is negotiated with an owner, not merely taken.

Same office, opposite valence: both are the master/mistress of the sea-game, but Sedna WITHHOLDS the seals in resentment until appeased, while Repun-kamuy freely drives the whales ashore and laughs off disrespect — the sea as grievance versus the sea as largesse.

Both are the detachable soul that animates a being and can depart or be freed at death; the Inuit tarniq and Ainu ramat are kindred North-Pacific soul-concepts, structurally close, no descent claimed.

A speculative reach: both locate spirit/awareness as a substance pervading the whole world rather than a class of beings, but the Inuit Sila is breath-weather-sentience encompassing all, where ramat is the indwelling soul each thing individually carries — flagged as loose.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The visceral, often terrifying soul journey undertaken by the ritual specialist to negotiate directly with the unseen powers.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The perilous, deep-ocean riches that are the ultimate, non-negotiable source of sustenance for human life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Female sovereigns of the cold ocean deeps who control the bounty of the sea and must be respected by fishers.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A powerful primordial goddess of the sea who commands the deep waters and holds dominion over the souls that pass through it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sovereign of the deep waters who directly controls the release of sea-life and fish to human beings.

Speculative: sky-burial's giving of the corpse back to the vultures rhymes faintly with circumpolar economies of the soul and body returned to the animal world; a loose functional parallel of corpse-as-offering, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both possess absolute dominion over marine mammals, functioning as deep-ocean masters who decide whether coastal communities eat or starve.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both institutionalize the shamanic journey to negotiate with spiritual powers, but the 'antap operate as a bureaucratic priesthood rather than solitary actors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The induction of an ecstatic, ego-less state to forcefully alter reality or gather hidden information.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as localized masters and literal hoarders of game animals who must be appeased or outwitted to release sustenance to human hunters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme sovereign of marine mammals dwelling at the bottom of the ocean, controlling the success of coastal hunters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An abstract, animating life-force and weather-principle pervading the harsh Arctic atmosphere.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: High-stakes Arctic shamanic practice involving intense physical and psychological transformation to mediate safely with hostile spirits.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions feature a tightly closed loop of reincarnation where souls are immediately reborn directly within their specific kin or community.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both rely on the deliberate detachment of the soul from the physical body to traverse unseen dimensions, though Eckankar aims for permanent spiritual liberation rather than immediate community intervention.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve an ecstatic specialist who uses rhythmic sound and trance to sever the soul from the body and navigate the spirit world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions recognize an invisible, lifelong spiritual companion or shade that provides the living individual with protection and identity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A spiritual practitioner who utilizes trance to physically project their soul into other realms to negotiate for the community.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A powerful keeper of the region's primary prey animals, demanding strict hunting taboos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A distinct, transferable soul-entity that governs vitality and is subject to spiritual theft or retrieval.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A terrifying female spirit residing in the depths who controls sustenance and demands appeasement through the hunt.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A myth of celestial domestic violence causing facial markings and an eternal chase, though the genders of the sun and moon are cleanly inverted.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An omnipresent supreme power deeply tied to the weather and daily survival in one of the earth's most extreme climates.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conception of the soul as a vulnerable, separable entity that can easily be lost, stolen, or frightened out of the physical body.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The perilous, ecstatic trance journey where the practitioner physically leaves the body to negotiate with spirit lords for a patient's life.

Both are the soul-traveller's power that walks between worlds to heal and divine — but the Inuit angakkuq flies the soul out where the Igbo dibịa reads the unseen through Agwụ's affa divination. A loose ecstatic-shaman echo, flagged.
Daughter and father, sea-mother and ferryman of the dead — the founding act of violence (the severed fingers) binds them into a single household cosmology.
The rite exists for the goddess: the shaman dives to comb Sedna's hair and release the game — the descent and the deity define each other.
Sila is the encompassing weather-order; the Sun-sister and Moon-brother are the visible reckoners of time turning within it.
Both turn on a descent to a watery/underworld realm to restore the flow of life; Sedna's undersea house and Inanna's underworld each gate the world's fertility behind a journey down.

Wounded, withholding queen of the deep whose grievance must be soothed before she releases what she holds — Ereshkigal's combed hair in the Inanna myth uncannily rhymes with the angakkuq combing Sedna's.
Both are mother-bodies from which sustenance is drawn, but Sedna is sea and resentment where Papatuanuku is earth and grief — a speculative pairing of provider-mothers wounded into their role.
The withholding mother whose mood is the world's scarcity: Demeter starves the earth in grief, Sedna stops the seals in anger — fertility hostage to a goddess's wound.
Both ferry the dead across water to the cold realm beneath — Anguta carrying souls down to Adlivun as Charon poles them over the Styx.
Both conduct and hold the dead in a place of atonement before release — the psychopomp as keeper of the threshold of judgement.
An impersonal, all-pervading order that is kept in relation rather than worshipped — Sila as breath-and-rightness rhymes with the Dao as the way the cosmos moves.

A speculative reach: Sila as encompassing world-awareness in which the self participates, faintly echoing Brahman as the pervading ground — but Sila is weather and breath, never a metaphysical absolute.
Both name a single all-pervading sacred power/process behind the many spirits rather than a personal high god — order as substance, not sovereign.
The shaman's detachable soul that journeys and can be lost rhymes with the Mesoamerican nagual — the self that travels apart from the body.
Two souls in one person — the vital double (tarniq) and the inherited name-soul (atiq) — together making identity a thing both vulnerable and reborn.

A female Sun fleeing into or out of the dark over a sibling's offence — Malina chased by her Moon-brother, Amaterasu withdrawing from Susanoo's violence; the sun gendered female and driven by family rupture.
Both personify the sun's daily course, but the Inuit Sun is a wronged sister fleeing rather than a charioteer riding in glory — the same body read through shame instead of majesty.

A loose reach: the game-master who owns the wild animals faintly parallels the orisa-owner of a domain, but Inuit master-spirits are keepers of consent in the hunt, not personal gods — flag as speculative.
GOLD SEAM: The Prophet's Night Journey (Mi'raj) through the heavens parallels the ecstatic shamanic ascent, though here used to finalize the universal law of prayer.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve the healer undertaking a dangerous spiritual pursuit to locate and recapture a patient's missing soul from malevolent forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both view the human essence not as a monolithic identity, but as an invisible companion or energetic layer vulnerable to spatial separation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A powerful female deity presiding over a domain considered taboo, managing the 'impurity' that male gods cannot touch.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A powerful, tempestuous goddess of the deep ocean who commands souls and controls maritime bounty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A semi-autonomous soul entity whose detachment or wandering from the physical body is the primary cause of illness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A specific soul-part that ensures reincarnation strictly within a given family lineage, effectively recycling ancestral identity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The physical, shadow or shade component of the soul that departs to the afterlife, distinct from the reincarnating essence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A remote, atmospheric supreme power governing the severe weather of the Arctic.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ecstatic Arctic specialists who enter deep trance to locate and retrieve lost human souls.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme supernatural owner of the primary Arctic herd animal (reindeer/caribou).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ritual practitioner forcefully bypasses ordinary consciousness, traveling through unseen metaphysical hierarchies to negotiate directly with cosmic forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both rely on the deliberate detachment of the practitioner's consciousness from the physical body to travel, negotiate, and gather information in unseen realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Inuit cosmology features the tarniq, a miniature soul that can be displaced or stolen, directly mirroring the neo-shamanic etiology of illness as soul fragmentation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A formidable sovereign of the watery abyss, commanding the terrifying and resource-rich depths of the sea.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve harrowing, psychologically shattering journeys across distinct cosmic spheres, though O9A seeks sociopathy rather than community healing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are rigorous sensory-deprivation trance states designed to detach the soul from the body for perilous spiritual travel.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Deep, maternal ocean spirits whose profound domain over the waters commands both immense reverence and fear.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The soul understood not as a monolith, but as a divisible, fragile entity that can leave the body and cause disease.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A woman betrayed by circumstance whose profound suffering and death transfigures her into a fearsome, unavoidable supernatural force.

Both the Arctic soul-traveller who leaves the body to fly the worlds and treat with the powers of game and death; the noaidi diverges by riding the herd-world itself (fish, bird, bull-reindeer), where the angakkuq descends to the sea-mother of the seals.

The bear treated with apology and re-fleshed so the game returns rhymes with the Inuit master of the land-animals who must be kept in good will for the hunt to renew — the hunter's debt to the owned wild, met across the Arctic by separate peoples.
GOLD SEAM: While the Inuit shaman literally travels to the spirit world, the Satanic magician uses ritual purely as an inward psychological tool to manipulate their own mind.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sovereign master of beasts who controls the fates of the creatures bound to his domain, demanding respect to release his bounty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A deeply aggrieved force that inflicts physical illness and ecological ruin upon a community until public confession and restitution are made.
GOLD SEAM: The medium's trance parallels the shamanic journey, temporarily vacating ordinary consciousness to bridge the material and spirit worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A mythic figure permanently exiled to the moon, whose lunar presence is the direct, visible result of a violent primordial transgression.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve a visionary practitioner deliberately and consciously traversing the unseen realms to acquire knowledge essential for the living.

Great water-mothers who hold the cycle of life and increase — Atabey of fresh water, moon and childbirth and Sedna of the sea-game in her undersea house — the female deep as the source, two unconnected coastal worlds.

Specialists who enter trance to consult the spirit-world for healing and the future — the behique inhaling cohoba at the zemí's dish and the angakkuq's soul-flight — the ecstatic-shaman reached in the Antilles and the Arctic alike.
GOLD SEAM: The shamanic katabasis—crossing physically into the spirit world or underworld through trance to retrieve a lost soul.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve a specific yogic or shamanic technique to breach gravity and physical laws to access and affect the deeper spiritual realms.

Two keepers-of-the-game who own and protect the prey — Anhangá the white deer of the forest-hunt and the Inuit Tekkeitsertok master of the caribou; the herd's owner who decides what the hunter may take, in tropic and arctic alike.

Both are female sovereigns of the deep into whom people pass and drown — but Sedna is the rationing mother of sea-game whose body is the ocean's law, while Iara is a luring drowner of men; flagged, the deep personified at opposite moral poles.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A delicate, autonomous inner soul whose misalignment or defilement is the primary cause of physical illness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying, demanding female sovereign of the waters who guards immense wealth and punishes human transgression.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A spiritual practitioner who utilizes trance to physically project their soul into perilous dimensions to heal the community.
GOLD SEAM: A tightly closed loop of reincarnation where souls are immediately reborn directly within their specific kin or ethnoreligious community.

dossier.essence: The traditional religion of the Inuit and Yupik peoples of the Arctic — Greenland, Arctic Canada, Alaska, and Chukotka — rooted in the Thule culture (c. 1000 CE) and older Paleo-Eskimo traditions. It is an animist, shamanic hunting religion without a creator god: the cosmos is full of persons (animals, weather, the dead) with whom humans must keep right relations, mediated by the angakkuq (shaman). Its central concern is the ethics of the hunt — that animals are owned by master-spirits and give themselves only if taboos are honored.
dossier.cosmos: The world is pervaded by Sila — breath, air, weather, and an encompassing moral awareness at once — so that a broken taboo troubles the very weather. The sea and its game belong to the Sea-Woman (Sedna / Nuliajuk / Takannaaluk), maimed into her role, who withholds the seals when human wrongdoing fouls her hair. Persons carry multiple souls, including a name-soul (atiq) by which a dead relative returns in the child who takes the name; the dead pass to realms beneath the sea or in the sky (e.g. Adlivun).
dossier.practice: The angakkuq, drumming into trance, sends the soul beneath the sea to comb Sedna's hair and release the game, retrieves stolen souls, and diagnoses the taboo-breaches behind sickness and dearth. Everyday religion is a web of hunting taboos and observances that keep animals willing to return, alongside amulets, songs, and the careful treatment of killed game. There is no priesthood, temple, or scripture — the specialist is the shaman, and observance is domestic and communal.
dossier.canon: A wholly oral tradition of myths, songs, and taboos; the principal (though mediated) written record is the early-20th-century ethnography of Knud Rasmussen's Fifth Thule Expedition, alongside later Yupik and Iñupiaq oral collections.
dossier.reflects: Morality written into the body of the prey — a cosmos with no surplus and no waste, where every kill is a transaction inside a web of consent and the order of the world is the ethics of eating.
sources: Knud Rasmussen, 'Intellectual Culture of the Iglulik Eskimos' (Report of the Fifth Thule Expedition, 1929)
Daniel Merkur, 'Becoming Half Hidden: Shamanism and Initiation among the Inuit' (1985)
Bernard Saladin d'Anglure, 'Être et renaître inuit, homme, femme ou chamane' (2006)
Ann Fienup-Riordan, 'Boundaries and Passages: Rule and Ritual in Yup'ik Eskimo Oral Tradition' (1994)
